**Papaverine** ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") *[papaver](/wiki/Papaver "Papaver")*, "poppy") is an [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium") [alkaloid](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") [antispasmodic](/wiki/Antispasmodic "Antispasmodic") drug, used primarily in the treatment of visceral [spasms](/wiki/Spasm "Spasm") and [vasospasms](/wiki/Vasospasm "Vasospasm") (especially those involving the intestines, heart, or brain), occasionally in the treatment of [erectile dysfunction](/wiki/Erectile_dysfunction "Erectile dysfunction") and [acute mesenteric ischemia](/wiki/Acute_mesenteric_ischemia "Acute mesenteric ischemia"). While it is found in the [opium poppy](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum"), papaverine differs in both structure and pharmacological action from the analgesic [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") and its derivatives (such as [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine")).

In addition to opium, papaverine is purported to be present in high concentrations in [star gooseberry](/wiki/Sauropus_androgynus "Sauropus androgynus").[[1]](#cite_note-99mTc-DTPA-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaverine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Opium_pod_cut_to_demonstrate_fluid_extraction1.jpg/250px-Opium_pod_cut_to_demonstrate_fluid_extraction1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opium_pod_cut_to_demonstrate_fluid_extraction1.jpg)

Capsule of *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")* showing latex (opium) exuding from incision. Papaverine occurs naturally in opium.

Papaverine was discovered in 1848 by Georg Merck (1825–1873).[[2]](#cite_note-2) Merck was a student of the German chemists [Justus von Liebig](/wiki/Justus_von_Liebig "Justus von Liebig") and [August Hofmann](/wiki/August_Wilhelm_von_Hofmann "August Wilhelm von Hofmann"), and he was the son of [Emanuel Merck](/wiki/Heinrich_Emanuel_Merck "Heinrich Emanuel Merck") (1794–1855), founder of the [Merck](/wiki/Merck_KGaA "Merck KGaA") corporation, a major German chemical and pharmaceutical company.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Biosynthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaverine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Biosynthesis")]

Papaverine is an [isoquinoline alkaloid](/wiki/Isoquinoline_alkaloids "Isoquinoline alkaloids") and the early steps in its biosynthesis from [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine") are well established.[[4]](#cite_note-Ullmann-4)[[5]](#cite_note-NPR-5) The final step is known to be catalysed by the enzyme [tetrahydroberberine oxidase](/wiki/Tetrahydroberberine_oxidase "Tetrahydroberberine oxidase"), which [oxidises](/wiki/Redox "Redox") (*S*)-tetrahydropapaverine:[[5]](#cite_note-NPR-5)[[6]](#cite_note-KEGG-6)

[![2D representation of the chemical structure of Q27465279.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/%28S%29-tetrahydropapaverine.svg/250px-%28S%29-tetrahydropapaverine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:(S)-tetrahydropapaverine.svg)

[(*S*)-tetrahydropapaverine](/w/index.php?title=Tetrahydropapaverine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tetrahydropapaverine (page does not exist)")

2 O2

2 H2O2

[![2D representation of the chemical structure of papaverine .](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Papaverine.svg/250px-Papaverine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Papaverine.svg)

papaverine

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Papaverine&action=edit), it is unclear whether (*S*)-tetrahydropapaverine derives from [(S)-reticuline](/wiki/Reticuline "Reticuline") or [(*S*)-coclaurine](/wiki/Coclaurine "Coclaurine"): the difference is whether the [piperidine](/wiki/Piperidine "Piperidine") nitrogen atom is [methylated](/wiki/Methyl_group "Methyl group") at any point during the biosynthetic sequence.[[5]](#cite_note-NPR-5)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaverine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Uses")]

Papaverine is approved to treat spasms of the gastrointestinal tract, [bile ducts](/wiki/Bile_duct "Bile duct") and [ureter](/wiki/Ureter "Ureter") and for use as a [cerebral](/wiki/Cerebrum "Cerebrum") and [coronary](/wiki/Coronary_circulation "Coronary circulation") [vasodilator](/wiki/Vasodilator "Vasodilator") in [subarachnoid hemorrhage](/wiki/Subarachnoid_hemorrhage "Subarachnoid hemorrhage") (combined with [balloon angioplasty](/wiki/Balloon_angioplasty "Balloon angioplasty"))[[7]](#cite_note-7) and [coronary artery bypass surgery](/wiki/Coronary_artery_bypass_surgery "Coronary artery bypass surgery").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Papaverine may also be used as a smooth muscle relaxant in [microsurgery](/wiki/Microsurgery "Microsurgery") where it is applied directly to blood vessels.

Papaverine is used as an [erectile dysfunction](/wiki/Erectile_dysfunction "Erectile dysfunction") drug, alone or sometimes in combination.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Papaverine, when injected in penile tissue, causes direct [smooth muscle](/wiki/Smooth_muscle "Smooth muscle") relaxation and consequent filling of the [corpus cavernosum](/wiki/Corpus_cavernosum_penis "Corpus cavernosum penis") with blood resulting in [erection](/wiki/Erection "Erection"). A [topical gel](/wiki/Topical_gels "Topical gels") is also available for ED treatment.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

It is also commonly used in [cryopreservation](/wiki/Cryopreservation "Cryopreservation") of [blood vessels](/wiki/Blood_vessels "Blood vessels") along with the other [glycosaminoglycans](/wiki/Glycosaminoglycan "Glycosaminoglycan") and protein suspensions.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Functions as a [vasodilator](/wiki/Vasodilator "Vasodilator") during cryopreservation when used in conjunction with [verapamil](/wiki/Verapamil "Verapamil"), [phentolamine](/wiki/Phentolamine "Phentolamine"), [nifedipine](/wiki/Nifedipine "Nifedipine"), [tolazoline](/wiki/Tolazoline "Tolazoline") or [nitroprusside](/wiki/Nitroprusside "Nitroprusside").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Papaverine is also being investigated as a topical growth factor in [tissue expansion](/wiki/Tissue_expansion "Tissue expansion") with some success.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Papaverine is used as an [off-label](/wiki/Off-label_use "Off-label use") [prophylaxis](/wiki/Prophylaxis "Prophylaxis") (preventative) of [migraine headaches](/wiki/Migraine_headaches "Migraine headaches").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) It is not a first line drug such as a few [beta blockers](/wiki/Beta_blockers "Beta blockers"), [calcium channel blockers](/wiki/Calcium_channel_blockers "Calcium channel blockers"), [tricyclic antidepressants](/wiki/Tricyclic_antidepressants "Tricyclic antidepressants"), and some [anticonvulsants](/wiki/Anticonvulsants "Anticonvulsants") such as [divalproex](/wiki/Divalproex "Divalproex"), but rather when these first line drugs and secondary drugs such as [SSRIs](/wiki/SSRIs "SSRIs"), [angiotensin II receptor antagonists](/wiki/Angiotensin_II_receptor_antagonist "Angiotensin II receptor antagonist"), etc. fail in the prophylaxis of migraines, have intolerable side effects or are contraindicated.

Papaverine is also present in combinations of opium alkaloid salts such as [papaveretum](/wiki/Papaveretum "Papaveretum") ([Omnopon](/wiki/Omnopon "Omnopon"), [Pantopon](/wiki/Pantopon "Pantopon")) and others, along with [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine"), and in some cases [noscapine](/wiki/Noscapine "Noscapine") and others in a percentage similar to that in opium, or modified for a given application.

Papaverine is found as a contaminant in some [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin")[[20]](#cite_note-20) and can be used by forensic laboratories in heroin profiling to identify its source.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The metabolites can also be found in the urine of heroin users, allowing street heroin to be distinguished from pharmaceutical [diacetylmorphine](/wiki/Diacetylmorphine "Diacetylmorphine").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Mechanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaverine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mechanism")]

The *in vivo* mechanism of action is not entirely clear, but an inhibition of the [enzyme](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") phosphodiesterase causing elevation of cyclic AMP and cyclic GMP[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] levels is significant. It may also alter [mitochondrial](/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria") [respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration").

Papaverine has also been demonstrated to be a selective [phosphodiesterase inhibitor](/wiki/Phosphodiesterase_inhibitor "Phosphodiesterase inhibitor") for the [PDE10A](/wiki/PDE10A "PDE10A") subtype found mainly in the [striatum](/wiki/Striatum "Striatum") of the brain. When administered chronically to mice, it produced motor and cognitive deficits and [increased anxiety](/wiki/Anxiogenic "Anxiogenic"), but conversely may produce an [antipsychotic](/wiki/Antipsychotic "Antipsychotic") effect,[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) although not all studies support this view.[[25]](#cite_note-Weber_2009_papaverine_PPI-25)

## Side effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaverine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Side effects")]

Frequent side effects of papaverine treatment include polymorphic [ventricular tachycardia](/wiki/Ventricular_tachycardia "Ventricular tachycardia"), constipation, interference with [sulphobromophthalein](/wiki/Sulphobromophthalein "Sulphobromophthalein")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] retention test (used to determine hepatic function), increased [transaminase](/wiki/Transaminase "Transaminase") levels, increased [alkaline phosphatase](/wiki/Alkaline_phosphatase "Alkaline phosphatase") levels, [somnolence](/wiki/Somnolence "Somnolence"), and [vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(medical) "Vertigo (medical)").

Rare side effects include flushing of the face, [hyperhidrosis](/wiki/Hyperhidrosis "Hyperhidrosis") (excessive sweating), [cutaneous eruption](/wiki/Drug_eruption "Drug eruption"), arterial [hypotension](/wiki/Hypotension "Hypotension"), [tachycardia](/wiki/Tachycardia "Tachycardia"), loss of appetite, [jaundice](/wiki/Jaundice "Jaundice"), [eosinophilia](/wiki/Eosinophilia "Eosinophilia"), [thrombopenia](/wiki/Thrombopenia "Thrombopenia"), mixed [hepatitis](/wiki/Hepatitis "Hepatitis"), headache, allergic reaction, chronic active hepatitis, and paradoxical aggravation of cerebral vasospasm.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Papaverine in the plant [Sauropus androgynus](/wiki/Sauropus_androgynus "Sauropus androgynus") is linked to [bronchiolitis obliterans](/wiki/Bronchiolitis_obliterans "Bronchiolitis obliterans").[[27]](#cite_note-pmid26413127-27)

## Formulations and trade names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Papaverine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Formulations and trade names")]

Papaverine is available in its salt form as the [hydrochloride](/wiki/Hydrochloride "Hydrochloride"), [codecarboxylate](/wiki/Pyridoxal_phosphate "Pyridoxal phosphate"), [adenylate](/wiki/Adenosine_monophosphate "Adenosine monophosphate"), and teprosylate. It was also once available as a salt of [hydrobromide](/wiki/Hydrobromide "Hydrobromide"), [camsylate](/wiki/Camphorsulfonic_acid "Camphorsulfonic acid"), cromesilate, [nicotinate](/wiki/Nicotinic_acid "Nicotinic acid"), and [phenylglycolate](/wiki/Mandelic_acid "Mandelic acid"). The hydrochloride salt is available for intramuscular, intravenous, rectal and oral administration. The teprosylate is available in intravenous, intramuscular, and orally administered formulations. The codecarboxylate is available in oral form, only, as is the adenylate.

The codecarboxylate is sold under the name Albatran,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the adenylate as Dicertan,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and the hydrochloride salt is sold variously as Artegodan (Germany), Cardioverina (countries outside Europe and the United States), Dispamil (countries outside Europe and the United States), Opdensit (Germany), Panergon (Germany), Paverina Houde (Italy, Belgium), Pavacap (United States), Pavadyl (United States), Papaverine (Israel), Papaverin-Hamelin (Germany), Paveron (Germany), Spasmo-Nit (Germany), Cardiospan, Papaversan, Cepaverin, Cerespan, Drapavel, Forpaven, Papalease, Pavatest, Paverolan, Therapav (Canada[[28]](#cite_note-28)), Vasospan, Cerebid, Delapav, Dilaves, Durapav, Dynovas, Optenyl, Pameion, Papacon, Pavabid, Pavacen, Pavakey, Pavased, Pavnell, Alapav, Myobid, Vasal, Pamelon, Pavadel, Pavagen, Ro-Papav, Vaso-Pav, Papanerin-hcl, Qua bid, Papital T.R., Paptial T.R., Pap-Kaps-150.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), papaverine and [homatropine methylbromide](/wiki/Homatropine_methylbromide "Homatropine methylbromide") are used in mild drugs that help "flush" the [bile](/wiki/Bile "Bile").[[29]](#cite_note-29)
